settings.title: A Warm Up That Actually Earns Its Place
settings.excerpt: Five minutes done well beats twenty minutes of half hearted stretching before you lift.
settings.body:
Most people either skip the warm up entirely or drift through fifteen minutes of stretching that does nothing for the session ahead. There is a better middle ground.

A warm up should raise your temperature, move the joints you are about to load, and rehearse the movement pattern. That is it.

What a warm up is actually for

The job is to get blood moving, free up the joints you are about to ask a lot of, and remind your body what the lift feels like at a light weight. Long static stretching before heavy work does none of that well, and can leave you feeling a little loose rather than ready.

For a squat day we use:

  • Two minutes on the bike or a brisk walk to get warm.
  • Some hip and ankle mobility while you breathe.
  • A couple of light sets of the lift itself, building toward your working weight.

Save the long stretching for later

If you enjoy a proper stretch, keep it for the end of the session or a rest day, when it does not blunt the work you came to do. A few minutes after training is a fine time to chase mobility.

The same three steps work for almost any lift. Swap the joint drills to match the movement, keep the ramp up sets light, and you are ready in roughly five minutes.

You will lift better and feel safer, and you will be done in five minutes. Ask us to walk you through it next session.
